title: 4 AFT Rejects and Unpostable Transactions
body_text:
4	AFT Rejects and Unpostable Transactions
4.1	Overview of AFT Rejects and Unpostable Transactions
A rejected AFT file or transaction is the result of an error. When an AFT file is rejected, it is because there are formatting issues in the file (e.g., incorrect File Creation Number, incorrect date, incorrect formatting). When an AFT transaction is rejected, it is because there is an error in the AFT transaction (e.g., incorrect transaction code, incorrect formatting, incorrect account number).
An unpostable credit or debit transaction occurs when a credit or debit transaction is sent to a financial institution and the transaction is rejected by the financial institution芒聙聶s banking system because of an invalid account number. The financial institution can either return the transaction or post it manually to its banking system. See CPA Rule F1, Rules Applicable to Automated Funds Transfer (AFT) Transactions, for more information.
4.2	Handling Rejected AFT Files
File validation is immediate upon uploading the file.
PaymentStream
AFT indicates if a file has errors. Errors must be corrected before the file can be validated.
It is the Originator芒聙聶s responsibility to ensure that the file is successfully received at Central 1. Originators that upload the file to
PaymentStream
AFT or originate transactions directly on
PaymentStream
AFT must verify that a confirmation email is received for each AFT file submitted or review the Activity and History Log that shows if the file was successfully validated. If a confirmation is not received or cannot be validated, the file must be corrected and retransmitted. Central 1 will not contact the Originator in these situations.
If the financial institution inputs transactions on behalf of a business member, the parties decide how Rejects, incoming returns, Error Corrections, etc. are communicated to the business member during the set-up process.
4.3	Handling Rejected AFT Transactions
AFT transactions are subject to a validation check both at Central 1 and at the data centre of the Receiving Financial Institution. Transactions that fail either of the validation checks are rejected back to the Originator.
Financial institution staff proceed as follows each business day:
Check the Consolidated Returns, Recalls, Rejects, and Error Corrections Report (ICRR/UCRR) daily to determine if any AFT transactions were rejected. If the financial institution enters transactions on behalf of a business member, the client must be notified of all Rejects and the client must provide correct information to the financial institution. Financial institutions can also check the Settlement Register ICSR/UCSR report, which specifies if Central 1 or another financial institution rejected the transaction.
Note:	See the AFT Reports Part for more information on these reports.
Rejected items will not be automatically posted on the ICRR/UCRR report. As a result, financial institutions must review the Rejected items and the Total Rejects field of the ICRR/UCRR report to determine which Rejected items require manual posting.
The Tran column indicates if the transaction is a debit or a credit. The Tran field includes the transaction type (3-digit code) followed by the logical record type (C, D, E, F, I, or J). If the transaction is Rejected, the number 芒聙聹900芒聙聺 (transaction type) will be displayed on the report. For debits the transaction type will be followed by the logical record types D, E, or J. For credits the transaction type will be followed by logical record types C, F, or I.
The 2-digit code in the Invalid Field No. column identifies the reason for Rejection. Both Central 1 and data centre Rejects are reported on the ICRR/UCRR report. The Settlement Register Report (ICSR/UCSR) specifies if Central 1 or another financial institution rejected the transaction.
Use the ICRR/UCRR report to post Rejected transactions back to the Originators芒聙聶 accounts on the Due Date. Post a Rejected credit as a credit and a Rejected debit as a debit.
The Originator proceeds as follows each business day:
Check the Consolidated Returns, Recalls, Rejects, and Error Corrections Report ICRR/UCRR report daily to determine if any transactions were rejected.
Obtain the correct account information from the member/customer (if applicable).
Update the appropriate record on
PaymentStream
AFT or on the software used to create transactions (if applicable).
Arrange for payment of the Rejected item or, with authorization from the member/customer, create a 1-time transaction.
4.4	Re-routing Unpostable Transactions
Account Number Changes
If a member芒聙聶s account type or account number has changed but the Originator has not been notified of the change, the incoming AFT transaction will not post automatically. The financial institution can re-route the credit or debit transaction to the member芒聙聶s new account. Any liability resulting from the re-routing is borne by the financial institution. Create a Notice of Change (NOC) transaction to advise the Originator of the correct account information so that future AFT transactions are automatically posted to the member芒聙聶s account. Alternatively, the financial institution can return the AFT transaction to the Originator. See CPA Rule F1, Rules Applicable to Automated Funds Transfer (AFT) Transactions, for more information.
Closed Branches
If a financial institution branch closes and the accounts are transferred to another branch, Central 1 automatically redirects settlement to the new branch if the status indicator on the Financial Institutions File is closed and cross referenced. It is recommended that the financial institution send a Notice of Change (NOC) transaction to the Originator. Settlement is redirected until the financial institution asks Central 1 to close the old branch on the CPA芒聙聶s Financial Institutions File (FIF). See CPA Rule F1, Rules Applicable to Automated Funds Transfer (AFT) Transactions, for more information.
Related Information
CPA Rule F1, Rules Applicable to Automated Funds Transfer (AFT) Transactions
END
